title: Buying Bread Before School
summary: A girl stops at a small French bakery on her way to school and chats with the baker about which bread to buy today.

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: Good morning! Can I have one baguette, please?

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: Good morning, Sara! Long one or short one today? We have both this morning.

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: A short one, please. My bag is small.

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: Here you go. It is very fresh. I made it ten minutes ago.

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: It smells wonderful! How much is it?

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: And here is your change. Eighty cents.

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: Thank you. Do you have any croissants today?

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: Yes, plain ones and chocolate ones. Which would you like?

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: One chocolate one, please. It is for my little sister.

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: That is one euro fifty. Total today is two euros seventy.

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: Oh, I do not have enough. I will leave the croissant.

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: No, no. You take it. You can pay me tomorrow.

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: Thank you so much! See you tomorrow!

annotations.exampleEn: He bought a long baguette from the bakery.
The bread is fresh from the oven.
How much is it for one apple?
He gave me five euros in change.
I prefer plain yogurt to fruit yogurt.
I do not have enough money for a taxi.

annotations.meaningVi: trả tiền cho tôi vào ngày mai
annotations.exampleEn: You can pay me tomorrow if you want.

grammarNotes.title: Polite shopping language
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi mua đồ ta thường dùng 'Can I have …, please?' để xin và 'How much is it?' để hỏi giá. Câu trả lời thường có 'Here you go.' (đưa đồ) và 'Here is your change.' (đưa tiền thừa). 'Thank you.' luôn được dùng sau khi nhận đồ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Shopping uses set phrases: 'Can I have …, please?' to ask, 'How much is it?' to ask the price, 'Here you go.' when handing something, 'Here is your change.' when giving money back, and 'Thank you.' after receiving.

grammarNotes.title: Saying prices in English
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Giá tiền nói theo công thức [số] + đơn vị tiền tệ + [số xu], ví dụ 'one euro and twenty' = €1.20, 'one euro fifty' = €1.50. Trong tiếng Anh ngày nay 'and' đôi khi được lược bỏ. Số nhỏ hơn một có thể dùng 'cents': 'Eighty cents.' = 0,80 €.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Prices are said as [number] + currency + [cents], e.g. 'one euro and twenty' = €1.20, 'one euro fifty' = €1.50. The word 'and' is often dropped. Amounts under one are said in cents: 'Eighty cents.' = €0.80.

translationVi: Sara: Chào buổi sáng! Cháu lấy một cái baguette ạ. Bác bán bánh: Chào buổi sáng Sara! Hôm nay cháu lấy cái dài hay cái ngắn? Bác bán bánh: Sáng nay bác có cả hai. Sara: Cháu lấy cái ngắn ạ. Cặp cháu nhỏ lắm. Bác bán bánh: Của cháu đây. Bánh rất tươi, bác mới làm cách đây mười phút. Sara: Thơm quá ạ! Bao nhiêu tiền vậy bác? Bác bán bánh: Một euro hai mươi cent. Sara: Cháu đưa bác hai euro. Bác bán bánh: Và đây là tiền thừa, tám mươi cent. Sara: Cảm ơn bác. Hôm nay có bánh sừng bò không ạ? Bác bán bánh: Có chứ, có loại thường và loại sô-cô-la. Cháu lấy loại nào? Sara: Cho cháu một cái sô-cô-la ạ. Cho em gái cháu. Bác bán bánh: Cái đó một euro năm mươi. Tổng cộng hôm nay là hai euro bảy mươi. Sara: Ôi, cháu không đủ tiền. Cháu để lại cái bánh sừng bò vậy. Bác bán bánh: Không, không. Cháu cứ cầm đi. Ngày mai trả bác cũng được. Sara: Cảm ơn bác nhiều lắm! Hẹn bác ngày mai!

questions.prompt: Does Sara choose the long or short baguette?
questions.explanation: 'A short one, please. My bag is small.'

questions.prompt: Why does she want the short one?
When did the baker make the bread?
How much change does Sara get?
What two types of croissant does the baker have?
Who is the chocolate croissant for?

options: Sara does not have enough money

questions.prompt: What is the main message of this dialogue?
options: The baker is kind to a young customer
questions.explanation: The baker lets Sara take the croissant and pay later.

cloze.template:
Sara: Good morning! Can I have one {{b1}}, please?
Baker: Good morning, Sara! Long one or short one today? We have both this morning.
Sara: A {{b2}} one, please. My bag is small.
Baker: Here you go. It is very {{b3}}. I made it ten minutes ago.
Sara: It smells {{b4}}! How much is it?
Baker: One {{b5}} and twenty, please.
Sara: Here are two euros.
Baker: And here is your {{b6}}. Eighty cents.
Sara: Thank you. Do you have any {{b7}} today?
Baker: Yes, plain ones and {{b8}} ones. Which would you like?
Sara: One chocolate one, please. It is for my little {{b9}}.
Baker: That is one euro fifty. Total today is two euros {{b10}}.
Sara: Oh, I do not have {{b11}}. I will leave the croissant.
Baker: No, no. You take it. You can pay me {{b12}}.
Sara: Thank you so much! See you tomorrow!

criticalThinkingQuestion: The baker lets Sara pay tomorrow. Why might small shops trust their regular customers? Would the same thing happen in a big supermarket?
